title: Resonance Walk
desc: Before the work crews enter Crystal Cave, someone has to walk the routes and listen for which formations are answering cleanly.

steps.text:
Crystal Cave never really looks still. Even when nothing moves, the red formations keep finding new ways to catch the light, throwing scarlet bands across the walls and turning every step into something half practical and half ceremonial. Before cutters, cleaners, or researchers enter for the day, the cave tenders make a full resonance walk. A clear-toned crystal is stable. A dead or muddy answer means a fracture, a loose support, or a section that should remain untouched until someone wiser looks at it.

The cave tender hands you a padded striker and a chalk slate. "Take the upper gallery if you want the wide notes," she says. "Take the lower reflection route if you want the brighter light and sharper footing. Either path tells us what the cave is willing to be today."

options.label: Walk the upper gallery and test the larger formations
Take the lower reflection route and read the brighter crystals

steps.text:
The upper gallery follows the edge of the main chamber, high enough that the whole cave opens below you in blades, pillars, and branching red fans. Each marked formation is tested with one light strike and a held breath. Clear ring, note it, move on. Two of them sing so cleanly the sound seems to stay in the air after the striker leaves your hand. One gives a shorter, muddier note with a faint rattle underneath.

You mark the bad tone in chalk and move on, the rest of the gallery answering like bells sunk into stone. By the end of the walk, you understand why the tenders never hurry this route. The cave is dangerous, yes, but it is also one of those places where danger has learned elegance. When you return the slate, the tender reads the marked fault and nods. "Good. One bad note found early saves a great deal of stupidity later."

options.label: Hand over the slate and leave the gallery ringing clear

steps.text:
The lower reflection route runs between tight crystal stands where polished mirrors and pale lamps bounce enough light ahead to keep the red walls from swallowing the whole passage. Here the formations are smaller, sharper, and bright enough at the edges to look hot even when the air is cold. You move carefully, sounding the route markers one by one and watching the reflections shift with each clear note.

Most answer high and clean. One cluster near a mirror post gives a flattened tone and shows a new hairline where two red branches meet. You chalk the fault, adjust the lamp angle so the next crew cannot miss it, and continue through the corridor while the whole route throws splintered light across your sleeves. When you come back, the tender checks your notes and smiles in the restrained way cave tenders allow themselves. "Good. Beautiful and usable is ideal. Usable alone will do."

options.label: Cap the striker and leave the lower route properly marked
